title: Otitis Externa
oneLiner: Acute infection of the external auditory canal, common in swimmers/humid climates; usually bacterial, but can be fungal. Rarely fatal but can become aggressive in immunocompromised patients.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Malignant (necrotising) otitis externa**  
  - Single finding: **granulation tissue at the bony-cartilaginous junction** + severe pain out of proportion + cranial nerve VII or lower CN palsies (especially CN VII, IX, X, XI).  
  - This is a skull base osteomyelitis (usually *Pseudomonas*); can be fatal if missed.  
  - **Must rule out** in diabetics, elderly, immunocompromised (HIV, chemotherapy, transplant).  
- **Mastoiditis**  
  - Single finding: **post-auricular swelling, tenderness, and pinna displaced forward** — not just canal pain.  
  - Can cause sigmoid sinus thrombosis, intracranial abscess. Needs CT and urgent ENT.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**  
  - Otalgia (often severe) – worse with chewing, yawning, **touching the pinna or tragus**  
  - Itching inside ear  
  - Discharge (yellow, green, rarely blood-stained)  
  - Hearing loss (conductive, from debris/swelling)  
  - Feeling of fullness/blockage  
- **Key exam findings:**  
  - **Tragal tenderness** – positive on manipulation  
  - Canal erythema, oedema, debris – may need **microsuction** to clear  
  - Tympanic membrane (TM) – usually normal (if visible) – intact, mobile  
  - If TM not visible: consider foreign body, furunculosis, or canal stenosis
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely → less likely:**  
  - Acute otitis media (AOM) – **pain on swallowing/opening mouth**, no tragal tenderness, TM bulging/opaque  
  - Foreign body – **history** (child, beach, cotton bud), unilateral discharge, visible object  
  - Furunculosis – **localised erythema, ‘pointing’ abscess** in lateral canal – similar to boil  
  - Ramsay Hunt syndrome (herpes zoster oticus) – vesicular rash in canal/concha + facial nerve palsy  
  - Cholesteatoma – chronic discharge, keratin pearl, ossicular erosion  
  - Contact dermatitis (e.g. from antibiotic drops) – **pruritus > pain**, bilateral, scaling  
- **Splitter:**  
  - **Tragal tenderness + normal TM = OE**  
  - **No tragal tenderness + abnormal TM = AOM**  
  (But note: OE can cause reflex TM hyperaemia – still intact and mobile.)
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**  
  - Otoscopy (after gentle suction if possible) – assess TM integrity  
  - **Tuning fork tests** (Rinne/Weber) – to distinguish conductive from sensorineural loss  
  - Swab for culture + sensitivity if:  
    - Refractory to first-line treatment  
    - Suspected fungal (drug-resistant)  
    - Malignant OE suspected  
- **Bloods:**  
  - FBC, CRP – if febrile, immunocompromised, or suspicion of spread  
  - ESR – elevated in malignant OE  
  - Blood cultures – if septic  
  - **Random blood glucose** – malignant OE is strongly associated with diabetes  
- **Imaging:**  
  - First-line: **CT temporal bones** – if malignant OE suspected (erosion of canal floor, skull base)  
  - Second-line: MRI temporal bones with contrast (better for soft tissue extent and CN involvement)  
- **Special tests:**  
  - Audiogram if hearing loss persists after treatment  
  - MRI brain/venogram if mastoiditis with intracranial complications  
- **Never-miss test:**  
  - **TM assessment** – if you suspect OE but TM is not visualised, **do not give aminoglycoside drops** (risk of ototoxicity if perforation present).  
  - **Blood glucose** in any severe/refractory case.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - **Gentle aural toilet** – microsuction (or dry mop with cotton wool wick) to remove debris and allow drops to penetrate  
  - **Analgesia** – paracetamol + NSAID (if no contraindication)  
  - **Topical antibiotic + steroid combination** – e.g. Sofradex (dexamethasone 0.05%, framycetin 0.5%, gramicidin) or Gentisone HC (gentamicin + hydrocortisone) or a quinolone drop (e.g. Ciloxan) if TM perforation suspected/unknown  
  - If canal is severely stenosed → insert a **wick** (e.g. Otomize, or a ribbon gauze soaked in the drop) to keep canal open for drops  
  - **No oral antibiotics** unless spreading cellulitis, fever, or malignant OE suspected  
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**  
  - **Rarely admitted** – admit only if:  
    - Malignant OE (IV antibiotics, CT, ENT consult)  
    - Severe cellulitis/fever requiring IV antibiotics  
    - Immunocompromised patient with failure of topical therapy  
    - Mastoiditis or intracranial complication  
  - If admitted:  
    - **IV antibiotics** (e.g. piperacillin-tazobactam or ciprofloxacin + gentamicin) – check local guidelines  
    - Microsuction daily by ENT  
    - Regular otoscopy + pain assessment  
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Complete 7-day course of topical antibiotic-steroid drops (up to 14 days if severe)  
  - If fungal (e.g. *Aspergillus*, *Candida*): use **clotrimazole 1% drops** or **miconazole** – avoid steroids  
  - Avoid water in ear for 1-2 weeks (swimming, showers – use cotton ball + petroleum jelly)  
  - ENT follow-up if:  
    - No improvement after 48-72h  
    - Recurrent episodes  
    - Suspected malignant OE  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - Fluids: IV 0.9% saline or Hartmann’s, maintenance rate  
  - VTE prophylaxis: if bedbound or high risk (e.g. diabetic, obese)  
  - Diet: normal  
  - Observations: NEWS 2-hourly if septic, otherwise 4-hourly
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**  
  - **Topical antibiotic + steroid**  
    - *Sofradex* (dexamethasone 0.05% / framycetin 0.5% / gramicidin) – 2-3 drops into affected ear TDS-QDS for 7 days  
    - *Gentisone HC* (gentamicin 0.3% / hydrocortisone 1%) – same dose  
    - *Otomize* (neomycin 0.5% / dexamethasone 0.05% / acetic acid) – 1 spray TDS  
    - *Ciloxan* (ciprofloxacin 0.3%) – 5 drops TDS for TM perforation or after grommet insertion  
  - **Wick** – e.g. *Otomize* spray, or ribbon gauze soaked in drop; change q24-48h  
  - **Analgesia** – paracetamol 1g PO QDS, ibuprofen 400mg PO TDS  
- **Common alternatives:**  
  - *Betamethadone-N* (betamethasone + neomycin)  
  - *Terra-Cortril* (oxytetracycline + hydrocortisone)  
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**  
  - **NEVER use aminoglycoside drops (gentamicin, neomycin, framycetin) if TM perforation suspected** – risk of cochlear/vestibular ototoxicity  
  - Avoid topical steroids if concurrent fungal infection (unless antifungal also used)  
  - Topical quinolones (ciprofloxacin) are safe in perforated TM but more expensive  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** none for topical drops  
- **Most dangerous dose error:**  
  - **Gentamicin or neomycin drops in an ear with TM perforation** – can cause permanent hearing loss or vertigo. If unknown TM status, use a quinolone drop (e.g. Ciloxan) or refer to ENT for microsuction and visualisation.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**  
  - Canal stenosis (meatal stenosis) from chronic inflammation and scarring  
  - Perichondritis / cellulitis of pinna  
  - Malignant (necrotising) otitis externa – skull base osteomyelitis, CN palsies, sepsis  
  - Intracranial extension (rare: meningitis, sinus thrombosis, brain abscess)  
- **Chronic:**  
  - Persistent discharge and hearing loss  
  - Cholesteatoma formation (rare)  
  - Recurrent episodes due to anatomical narrowing or contact dermatitis
sections.Prognosis:
- **Excellent** – >90% resolve within 7-10 days with appropriate topical treatment.  
- Malignant OE has high morbidity (CN deficits) and mortality (10-20%) if untreated – early recognition and aggressive IV antibiotics + surgical debridement improve outcome.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always check TM before prescribing any drop** – if TM not seen, use quinolone drop or refer for microsuction.  
- **Use a wick** if canal is severely swollen – it keeps medication in contact with the skin.  
- **Advise “keep the ear dry”** – avoid swimming, use a shower cap, and do not use cotton buds.  
- **Treat the itch** – oral antihistamines (e.g. cetirizine 10mg ON) can help, especially in allergic/eczematous cases.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Prescribing oral antibiotics for simple otitis externa** – they do not reach adequate concentration in the canal and increase antibiotic resistance.  
- **Using aminoglycoside drops without knowing TM status** – assume perforation until proven otherwise.  
- **Forgetting to suction debris** – drops cannot penetrate a canal full of discharge.  
- **Giving topical steroids alone** if fungal infection is suspected (e.g. previous antibiotics, itchy, white debris) – do a KOH mount or swab.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**  
  1. Pain becomes severe or spreads to cheek/neck  
  2. Facial weakness, dizziness, or hearing loss worsens  
  3. Fever >38.5°C or redness extends beyond ear canal  
  4. No improvement after 48 hours of topical treatment  
- **Follow-up:**  
  - GP in 7 days if symptoms resolved  
  - ENT clinic if refractory, recurrent, or suspected malignant OE
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / ENT registrar if:**  
  - NEWS2 ≥5  
  - Lactate >2 mmol/L, CRP >150, or signs of sepsis  
  - GCS drop, meningism, or focal neurology (urgent CT head + neurosurgery)  
  - **Any suspicion of malignant OE** – especially in diabetics/immunocompromised: granulation tissue, CN palsy, persistent pain despite treatment  
  - Mastoiditis: post-auricular swelling, pinna displacement  
- **Thresholds:**  
  - NEWS2 ≥5 → immediate review by reg/consultant  
  - Any cranial nerve palsy → urgent ENT + CT temporal bones  
  - Worsening otoscopy despite topical therapy → senior review for IV antibiotics and imaging

rawMarkdown:
## Otitis Externa
**One-liner:** Acute infection of the external auditory canal, common in swimmers/humid climates; usually bacterial, but can be fungal. Rarely fatal but can become aggressive in immunocompromised patients.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Malignant (necrotising) otitis externa**  
  - Single finding: **granulation tissue at the bony-cartilaginous junction** + severe pain out of proportion + cranial nerve VII or lower CN palsies (especially CN VII, IX, X, XI).  
  - This is a skull base osteomyelitis (usually *Pseudomonas*); can be fatal if missed.  
  - **Must rule out** in diabetics, elderly, immunocompromised (HIV, chemotherapy, transplant).  
- **Mastoiditis**  
  - Single finding: **post-auricular swelling, tenderness, and pinna displaced forward** — not just canal pain.  
  - Can cause sigmoid sinus thrombosis, intracranial abscess. Needs CT and urgent ENT.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**  
  - Otalgia (often severe) – worse with chewing, yawning, **touching the pinna or tragus**  
  - Itching inside ear  
  - Discharge (yellow, green, rarely blood-stained)  
  - Hearing loss (conductive, from debris/swelling)  
  - Feeling of fullness/blockage  
- **Key exam findings:**  
  - **Tragal tenderness** – positive on manipulation  
  - Canal erythema, oedema, debris – may need **microsuction** to clear  
  - Tympanic membrane (TM) – usually normal (if visible) – intact, mobile  
  - If TM not visible: consider foreign body, furunculosis, or canal stenosis

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely → less likely:**  
  - Acute otitis media (AOM) – **pain on swallowing/opening mouth**, no tragal tenderness, TM bulging/opaque  
  - Foreign body – **history** (child, beach, cotton bud), unilateral discharge, visible object  
  - Furunculosis – **localised erythema, ‘pointing’ abscess** in lateral canal – similar to boil  
  - Ramsay Hunt syndrome (herpes zoster oticus) – vesicular rash in canal/concha + facial nerve palsy  
  - Cholesteatoma – chronic discharge, keratin pearl, ossicular erosion  
  - Contact dermatitis (e.g. from antibiotic drops) – **pruritus > pain**, bilateral, scaling  
- **Splitter:**  
  - **Tragal tenderness + normal TM = OE**  
  - **No tragal tenderness + abnormal TM = AOM**  
  (But note: OE can cause reflex TM hyperaemia – still intact and mobile.)

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**  
  - Otoscopy (after gentle suction if possible) – assess TM integrity  
  - **Tuning fork tests** (Rinne/Weber) – to distinguish conductive from sensorineural loss  
  - Swab for culture + sensitivity if:  
    - Refractory to first-line treatment  
    - Suspected fungal (drug-resistant)  
    - Malignant OE suspected  
- **Bloods:**  
  - FBC, CRP – if febrile, immunocompromised, or suspicion of spread  
  - ESR – elevated in malignant OE  
  - Blood cultures – if septic  
  - **Random blood glucose** – malignant OE is strongly associated with diabetes  
- **Imaging:**  
  - First-line: **CT temporal bones** – if malignant OE suspected (erosion of canal floor, skull base)  
  - Second-line: MRI temporal bones with contrast (better for soft tissue extent and CN involvement)  
- **Special tests:**  
  - Audiogram if hearing loss persists after treatment  
  - MRI brain/venogram if mastoiditis with intracranial complications  
- **Never-miss test:**  
  - **TM assessment** – if you suspect OE but TM is not visualised, **do not give aminoglycoside drops** (risk of ototoxicity if perforation present).  
  - **Blood glucose** in any severe/refractory case.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - **Gentle aural toilet** – microsuction (or dry mop with cotton wool wick) to remove debris and allow drops to penetrate  
  - **Analgesia** – paracetamol + NSAID (if no contraindication)  
  - **Topical antibiotic + steroid combination** – e.g. Sofradex (dexamethasone 0.05%, framycetin 0.5%, gramicidin) or Gentisone HC (gentamicin + hydrocortisone) or a quinolone drop (e.g. Ciloxan) if TM perforation suspected/unknown  
  - If canal is severely stenosed → insert a **wick** (e.g. Otomize, or a ribbon gauze soaked in the drop) to keep canal open for drops  
  - **No oral antibiotics** unless spreading cellulitis, fever, or malignant OE suspected  
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**  
  - **Rarely admitted** – admit only if:  
    - Malignant OE (IV antibiotics, CT, ENT consult)  
    - Severe cellulitis/fever requiring IV antibiotics  
    - Immunocompromised patient with failure of topical therapy  
    - Mastoiditis or intracranial complication  
  - If admitted:  
    - **IV antibiotics** (e.g. piperacillin-tazobactam or ciprofloxacin + gentamicin) – check local guidelines  
    - Microsuction daily by ENT  
    - Regular otoscopy + pain assessment  
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Complete 7-day course of topical antibiotic-steroid drops (up to 14 days if severe)  
  - If fungal (e.g. *Aspergillus*, *Candida*): use **clotrimazole 1% drops** or **miconazole** – avoid steroids  
  - Avoid water in ear for 1-2 weeks (swimming, showers – use cotton ball + petroleum jelly)  
  - ENT follow-up if:  
    - No improvement after 48-72h  
    - Recurrent episodes  
    - Suspected malignant OE  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - Fluids: IV 0.9% saline or Hartmann’s, maintenance rate  
  - VTE prophylaxis: if bedbound or high risk (e.g. diabetic, obese)  
  - Diet: normal  
  - Observations: NEWS 2-hourly if septic, otherwise 4-hourly

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**  
  - **Topical antibiotic + steroid**  
    - *Sofradex* (dexamethasone 0.05% / framycetin 0.5% / gramicidin) – 2-3 drops into affected ear TDS-QDS for 7 days  
    - *Gentisone HC* (gentamicin 0.3% / hydrocortisone 1%) – same dose  
    - *Otomize* (neomycin 0.5% / dexamethasone 0.05% / acetic acid) – 1 spray TDS  
    - *Ciloxan* (ciprofloxacin 0.3%) – 5 drops TDS for TM perforation or after grommet insertion  
  - **Wick** – e.g. *Otomize* spray, or ribbon gauze soaked in drop; change q24-48h  
  - **Analgesia** – paracetamol 1g PO QDS, ibuprofen 400mg PO TDS  
- **Common alternatives:**  
  - *Betamethadone-N* (betamethasone + neomycin)  
  - *Terra-Cortril* (oxytetracycline + hydrocortisone)  
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**  
  - **NEVER use aminoglycoside drops (gentamicin, neomycin, framycetin) if TM perforation suspected** – risk of cochlear/vestibular ototoxicity  
  - Avoid topical steroids if concurrent fungal infection (unless antifungal also used)  
  - Topical quinolones (ciprofloxacin) are safe in perforated TM but more expensive  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** none for topical drops  
- **Most dangerous dose error:**  
  - **Gentamicin or neomycin drops in an ear with TM perforation** – can cause permanent hearing loss or vertigo. If unknown TM status, use a quinolone drop (e.g. Ciloxan) or refer to ENT for microsuction and visualisation.

### Complications
- **Acute:**  
  - Canal stenosis (meatal stenosis) from chronic inflammation and scarring  
  - Perichondritis / cellulitis of pinna  
  - Malignant (necrotising) otitis externa – skull base osteomyelitis, CN palsies, sepsis  
  - Intracranial extension (rare: meningitis, sinus thrombosis, brain abscess)  
- **Chronic:**  
  - Persistent discharge and hearing loss  
  - Cholesteatoma formation (rare)  
  - Recurrent episodes due to anatomical narrowing or contact dermatitis

### Prognosis
- **Excellent** – >90% resolve within 7-10 days with appropriate topical treatment.  
- Malignant OE has high morbidity (CN deficits) and mortality (10-20%) if untreated – early recognition and aggressive IV antibiotics + surgical debridement improve outcome.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always check TM before prescribing any drop** – if TM not seen, use quinolone drop or refer for microsuction.  
- **Use a wick** if canal is severely swollen – it keeps medication in contact with the skin.  
- **Advise “keep the ear dry”** – avoid swimming, use a shower cap, and do not use cotton buds.  
- **Treat the itch** – oral antihistamines (e.g. cetirizine 10mg ON) can help, especially in allergic/eczematous cases.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Prescribing oral antibiotics for simple otitis externa** – they do not reach adequate concentration in the canal and increase antibiotic resistance.  
- **Using aminoglycoside drops without knowing TM status** – assume perforation until proven otherwise.  
- **Forgetting to suction debris** – drops cannot penetrate a canal full of discharge.  
- **Giving topical steroids alone** if fungal infection is suspected (e.g. previous antibiotics, itchy, white debris) – do a KOH mount or swab.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**  
  1. Pain becomes severe or spreads to cheek/neck  
  2. Facial weakness, dizziness, or hearing loss worsens  
  3. Fever >38.5°C or redness extends beyond ear canal  
  4. No improvement after 48 hours of topical treatment  
- **Follow-up:**  
  - GP in 7 days if symptoms resolved  
  - ENT clinic if refractory, recurrent, or suspected malignant OE

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / ENT registrar if:**  
  - NEWS2 ≥5  
  - Lactate >2 mmol/L, CRP >150, or signs of sepsis  
  - GCS drop, meningism, or focal neurology (urgent CT head + neurosurgery)  
  - **Any suspicion of malignant OE** – especially in diabetics/immunocompromised: granulation tissue, CN palsy, persistent pain despite treatment  
  - Mastoiditis: post-auricular swelling, pinna displacement  
- **Thresholds:**  
  - NEWS2 ≥5 → immediate review by reg/consultant  
  - Any cranial nerve palsy → urgent ENT + CT temporal bones  
  - Worsening otoscopy despite topical therapy → senior review for IV antibiotics and imaging
